question_text: Staffing is primarily concerned with:
explanation: Staffing involves finding the right people for the right jobs. It includes recruitment, selection, placement, training, development, and performance appraisal of personnel in the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Setting goals and deciding how to achieve them
Finding and keeping the right people for the right jobs
Comparing actual performance with planned targets
Grouping activities into departments

elimination_hints.hint: Setting goals is planning, not staffing.
Comparing performance with targets is controlling.
Grouping activities into departments is organising.

question_text: Which of the following is NOT a step in the staffing process?
explanation: Budgeting is a function of planning, not staffing. The staffing process includes estimating manpower requirements, recruitment, selection, placement, orientation, training, development, performance appraisal, promotion, and compensation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Recruitment
Selection
Training
Budgeting

elimination_hints.hint: Recruitment is a core step in staffing — searching for prospective candidates.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking recruitment is not part of staffing

elimination_hints.hint: Selection is choosing the best candidate from applicants — clearly part of staffing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking selection is not part of staffing

elimination_hints.hint: Training and development of employees is a key staffing function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking training is not part of staffing

question_text: Staffing function begins with:
explanation: The staffing process starts with estimating the manpower requirements of the organisation. Only after knowing how many and what type of people are needed can recruitment, selection, and other steps follow.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Estimating the manpower requirements
Placing advertisements in newspapers
Conducting interviews of candidates
Providing on-the-job training

elimination_hints.hint: Advertisements come during recruitment, which happens after estimating requirements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting with recruitment instead of planning

elimination_hints.hint: Interviews are part of selection, which comes after recruitment.
Training comes much later, after the employee has been selected and placed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the sequence of staffing steps

question_text: Recruitment is defined as:
explanation: Recruitment is the process of searching for prospective employees and stimulating them to apply for jobs in the organisation. It creates a pool of applicants from which the best can be selected.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Choosing the best candidate from a pool of applicants
Placing selected candidates on appropriate jobs
Searching for prospective employees and stimulating them to apply
Evaluating employee performance against standards

elimination_hints.hint: Choosing the best candidate is selection, not recruitment.
Placing candidates on jobs is placement, which follows selection.
Evaluating performance is performance appraisal, a later staffing step.

question_text: Which of the following is an internal source of recruitment?
explanation: Transfer involves shifting an employee from one job, section, department, or shift to another within the same organisation. It is an internal source as the employee is already part of the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Campus recruitment
Transfer
Employment exchange
Web publishing (online recruitment)

elimination_hints.hint: Campus recruitment involves visiting educational institutions — an external source.
Employment exchanges are government agencies that provide external candidates.
Web publishing attracts candidates from outside the organisation.

question_text: Promotion as an internal source of recruitment means:
explanation: Promotion involves shifting an employee to a higher position carrying higher responsibilities, facilities, status, and pay. It is a form of internal recruitment as the person is already working in the organisation.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Shifting an employee to a higher position with greater responsibilities and pay
Moving an employee to a different department at the same level
Hiring a new employee from outside the organisation
Sending an employee for external training

elimination_hints.hint: Moving to a different department at the same level is transfer, not promotion.
Hiring from outside is external recruitment, not promotion.
Sending for training is a development activity, not promotion.

question_text: Which of the following is an external source of recruitment?
explanation: Direct recruitment involves putting up a notice on the notice board of the enterprise or at factory gate specifying details of jobs available. However, the question asks about external sources. Campus placement involves visiting educational institutions to recruit fresh graduates, which is an external source.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Promotion of existing employees
Transfer within the organisation
Re-employment of former employees
Campus placement

elimination_hints.hint: Promotion is an internal source — the employee already works in the organisation.
Transfer moves an existing employee — it is an internal source.
Re-employment of former employees can be considered internal as they were previously with the organisation.

question_text: Selection is described as a:
explanation: Selection is a negative process because it involves rejection of unsuitable candidates. Out of many applicants, only the most suitable are chosen, and the rest are rejected.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Positive process because it attracts more candidates
Negative process because it involves rejection of candidates
Neutral process with no impact on candidates
Creative process that generates new job roles

elimination_hints.hint: Attracting more candidates is the nature of recruitment (positive process), not selection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing nature of selection with recruitment

elimination_hints.hint: Selection clearly impacts candidates — some are accepted, others rejected.
Creating new job roles is part of organising, not selection.

question_text: The correct sequence in the selection process is:
explanation: The selection process follows this order: preliminary screening, selection tests, employment interview, reference and background checks, selection decision, medical examination, job offer, and contract of employment.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Preliminary screening → Selection tests → Interview → Reference check → Job offer
Interview → Selection tests → Preliminary screening → Job offer → Reference check
Selection tests → Interview → Job offer → Preliminary screening → Reference check
Reference check → Preliminary screening → Interview → Selection tests → Job offer

elimination_hints.hint: Preliminary screening comes first to filter out obviously unqualified applicants, not interviews.
Preliminary screening must come before tests — you can't test without first filtering applications.
Reference checks happen after interview, not as the first step.

question_text: Training refers to:
explanation: Training is the process of increasing the knowledge, skills, and abilities of employees for doing a particular job. It is a short-term process aimed at improving current job performance.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: A long-term process focused on overall growth of an employee
Evaluating employee performance against set targets
Increasing knowledge, skills, and abilities for doing a particular job
Deciding the compensation package for employees

elimination_hints.hint: Long-term overall growth describes development, not training. Training is short-term and job-specific.
Evaluating performance is performance appraisal, not training.
Deciding compensation is part of remuneration, not training.

question_text: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes between training and development?
explanation: Training is a short-term, job-specific process focused on improving performance in the current role, while development is a long-term, career-oriented process aimed at overall growth of the employee for future roles.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Training is for managers; development is for workers
Training is job-specific and short-term; development is career-oriented and long-term
Training increases salary; development does not
Training is external; development is internal

elimination_hints.hint: Training can be given to all levels, not just workers. The distinction is about scope and duration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking training/development differ by employee level

elimination_hints.hint: Neither training nor development are defined by their effect on salary.
Both training and development can use internal or external methods.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking source differentiates training and development

question_text: An advantage of internal sources of recruitment is:
explanation: Internal recruitment boosts employee morale because employees see opportunities for growth within the organisation. It motivates them to improve performance, knowing that good work can lead to promotion.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Employees are motivated as they see opportunities for advancement
It brings fresh talent and new ideas into the organisation
It provides a wider choice of candidates
It helps in attracting qualified candidates from the market

elimination_hints.hint: Fresh talent and new ideas come from external sources, not internal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing advantages of internal and external sources

elimination_hints.hint: Wider choice is an advantage of external recruitment — internal has a limited pool.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking internal sources provide wider choice

elimination_hints.hint: Attracting from the market means external sources.

question_text: A limitation of external sources of recruitment is:
explanation: External recruitment is generally a lengthy and expensive process involving advertising, screening large numbers of applications, conducting multiple rounds of tests and interviews, and verification of credentials.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: It leads to inbreeding and lack of innovation
It reduces the morale of existing employees
It is a lengthy and expensive process
It limits the choice of suitable candidates

elimination_hints.hint: Inbreeding is a limitation of internal sources — external recruitment actually brings fresh talent.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing internal source limitations to external

elimination_hints.hint: Reducing morale of existing employees is a limitation of external sources, but the most prominent limitation is cost and time.
elimination_hints.misconception: While partly true, this is not the primary limitation

elimination_hints.hint: External recruitment actually provides a wider choice, not a limited one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing advantage of external with limitation

question_text: Vestibule training is a method of training in which:
explanation: Vestibule training involves training employees on equipment and methods similar to actual ones used at the workplace, but in a separate training centre away from the actual work floor. It is useful where on-the-job training is too costly or dangerous.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Employees learn by actually performing the job under supervision
Employees are trained on equipment in a simulated environment away from the workplace
Employees attend classroom lectures at a university
Employees rotate through different departments periodically

elimination_hints.hint: Learning by actually performing the job is on-the-job training / apprenticeship.
Classroom lectures at a university is an off-the-job method but not vestibule training specifically.
Rotating through departments is job rotation, an on-the-job method.

question_text: Which of the following is an on-the-job training method?
explanation: Apprenticeship programmes are on-the-job training methods where a trainee (apprentice) is placed under the guidance of a master worker to learn a craft or trade while working. Internship, vestibule training, and classroom lectures are off-the-job methods.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Apprenticeship programme
Vestibule training
Classroom lectures
Case study method

elimination_hints.hint: Vestibule training uses a separate simulated environment — it is off-the-job.
Classroom lectures take place away from the actual workplace — off-the-job method.
Case study method is an off-the-job training technique used in management education.

question_text: Recruitment is considered a __________ process while selection is a __________ process.
explanation: Recruitment is a positive process because its purpose is to attract maximum number of candidates to apply. Selection is a negative process because it involves rejection of unsuitable candidates from the pool.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Negative; positive
Internal; external
Positive; negative
Formal; informal

elimination_hints.hint: It's the other way around — recruitment attracts (positive), selection rejects (negative).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the nature of recruitment and selection

elimination_hints.hint: Internal/external describes sources of recruitment, not the nature of the processes.
Formal/informal is not how recruitment and selection are distinguished.

question_text: Which selection test measures an individual's potential for learning new skills?
explanation: Aptitude test measures the potential of an individual to learn new skills. It indicates a person's capacity and ability to learn and acquire skills needed for a job, rather than measuring existing knowledge.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Intelligence test
Aptitude test
Personality test
Trade test

elimination_hints.hint: Intelligence test measures general mental ability (IQ), not potential to learn specific skills.
Personality test measures traits like introversion/extroversion, not learning potential.
Trade test measures existing proficiency in a specific trade, not potential to learn.

question_text: Induction training is given to a new employee to:
explanation: Induction (orientation) training familiarises a new employee with the organisation — its history, objectives, policies, rules, procedures, and the people they will work with. It helps the newcomer settle into the new environment.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Familiarise them with the organisation, its policies, rules, and co-workers
Teach them advanced managerial skills for future leadership roles
Test whether they are suitable for the job before confirmation
Evaluate their performance for the first salary increment

elimination_hints.hint: Teaching advanced managerial skills is development, not induction training.
Testing suitability is part of selection/probation, not induction.
Performance evaluation for increment is appraisal, not induction.

question_text: Which of the following is a benefit of training to the organisation?
explanation: Training leads to fewer accidents because employees learn the correct way of performing tasks and handling equipment. Well-trained employees are more efficient and make fewer mistakes, reducing workplace accidents.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Employees get higher salaries immediately
It eliminates the need for supervision entirely
It guarantees promotion for all trained employees
It leads to fewer accidents and reduced wastage

elimination_hints.hint: Training does not automatically lead to immediate salary increases.
elimination_hints.misconception: Linking training directly to salary hike

elimination_hints.hint: Training reduces the need for close supervision but does not eliminate it entirely.
Training improves skills but does not guarantee promotion for everyone.

question_text: Staffing is closely related to which other function of management?
explanation: Staffing is closely related to organising because the organisational structure determines the kind and number of people required. Organising creates positions and defines authority relationships; staffing fills those positions with suitable people.
subject: cuet-business-studies

options.text: Planning
Organising
Controlling
Directing

elimination_hints.hint: While planning determines objectives, staffing is most directly linked to organising which creates positions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking staffing is closest to planning

elimination_hints.hint: Controlling deals with measuring performance — it uses staffing outcomes but is not most closely related.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking staffing is closest to controlling

elimination_hints.hint: Directing involves leading and motivating — it comes after staffing but is not most closely linked.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking staffing is closest to directing
